steps.description: Rewrite the numerator in sine and cosine
steps.explanation: Converting the reciprocal functions is the first step towards combining the fraction.

steps.description: Combine the numerator over a common denominator
steps.explanation: Adding the two fractions gives a single numerator $\sin\theta + \cos\theta$.

steps.description: Rewrite the denominator of the main fraction
steps.explanation: Expressing the denominator over cosine reveals the same $\sin\theta + \cos\theta$ factor that appears in the numerator.

steps.description: Write the whole expression as a division
steps.explanation: Substituting both rewritten parts sets up a division of two fractions.

steps.description: Divide by multiplying by the reciprocal
steps.explanation: Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal.
Since $\sin\theta + \cos\theta = \cos\theta + \sin\theta$, that factor cancels top and bottom.
The remaining cosine cancels, leaving one over sine.
One over sine is cosecant, matching the right-hand side and completing the proof.
